seoTitle: Fractional PM or PO: half-days, AI, and ceremonies | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: Technical PM/PO fractional: regular half-days (~4 per week or fewer), full day when needed (at most once a week), priorities, prerequisites, and dev ceremonies.

hero.title: PM or PO need, without full-time
hero.lede:
You need a PM or PO, without a 40-hour hire. The team ships code; what's missing is quarterly prioritization, clear prerequisites, dev rituals, and hypotheses tested before build. It's not « too early for a PM », it's too heavy or too rigid full-time.

I work as a technical PM/PO: development, business logic, technical trade-offs, and product sense (background in design, then engineering, then product leadership). In the AI era, that combination is a real edge to frame, prototype, and decide without losing your team.

That fits as fractional support: half-days first, aligned to your rituals (stand-up, backlog, prerequisites), about four half-days per week or fewer depending on phase, plus a full day when needed (in practice, at most once a week) to prototype or validate before the dev team commits.

blocks.title: Product context
blocks.intro: The team ships code; what's often missing is a clear product line between the roadmap and the sprint. Two useful reads before rhythm and responsibilities.

panels.heading: When developers wait on product decisions
items: The founder arbitrates between customer fires, tech debt, and « the next big feature »
Devs move tickets without an explicit why or order
Each quarter, the roadmap looks like a wish list, not a plan the team can hold

panels.heading: Technical PM/PO: product, design, and code
items: Designer → developer → product path, not a generalist PM far from the codebase
Credible with devs, clear for the business each quarter
AI to test hypotheses in days, not months of fuzzy specs

blocks.title: Covering a PM without full-time
blocks.lede: Not a « four full days per week » rhythm: half-days as the default, and a full day when you need to go deeper, without a fixed 40-hour role.

items.title: Regular half-days (~4 per week, or fewer)
items.content: Stand-up in the morning, backlog review in the afternoon, prerequisite workshops, quarter sync: the calendar fits your team. In practice, about four half-days per week, sometimes fewer when the phase allows.

items.title: Full day when needed, not by default
items.content: When several hypotheses or features must be evaluated, prototyped, or tested before build: AI prototypes, clickable flows, light POCs. In practice, at most one full day per week, not four full days every week.

items.title: Senior technical PM/PO, not an employee
items.content: Same person for prioritization, prerequisites, UX, and technical judgment, without a handoff between « the PM » and « someone who gets the code ». Scope and rhythm adjustable without hiring or layoffs.

blocks.title: Four responsibilities a strong PM/PO owns

items.title: Quarterly prioritization
items.content: Quarter goals, explicit trade-offs, what you won't do as much as what you will. A review rhythm (monthly or quarterly) the team understands and the business can defend.

items.title: Prerequisites and ready-to-build
items.content: User stories, acceptance criteria, dependencies, open questions, before devs spend days on ambiguity. Fewer loops, fewer « we misunderstood » at sprint end.

items.title: AI prototypes before full build
items.content: Technical PM/PO and comfortable with AI: clickable flows, interactive mocks, light POCs, with design eye and business rigor to validate hypotheses before committing weeks of development.

items.title: Daily ceremonies with developers
items.content: Stand-up, unblocking, sprint alignment: someone who facilitates and keeps rituals on track, follows impediments, and connects product priority to what's happening in code.

blocks.title: Full-time PM/PO vs fractional support
blocks.intro: Ballpark, exact calendar (half-days vs full days) is set on a call. For AI in the product or launch framing, see also AI in your product and Launch a SaaS or MVP.

headers.fractionalLead: Technical PM/PO, senior product engineer (~4 half-days / week, 1 full day max when needed)

rows.fullTimePm: 5 days; fixed role even in quieter weeks.
rows.fractionalLead: ~4 half-days per week as the default (or fewer); occasional full day (in practice, at most once a week) for prototyping or validation.

rows.fullTimePm: Varies; frequent handoffs with the dev team.
rows.fractionalLead: Technical PM/PO: business logic, code, debt, APIs; same language as your devs.

rows.fullTimePm: Often slides or Figma; weak link to build reality.
rows.fractionalLead: Design + dev background; AI prototypes and user validation before build.

rows.fullTimePm: Salary + benefits; costly to reposition if the product pivots.
rows.fractionalLead: Fractional budget; intensity scaled up or down without layoffs.
Half-days aligned to stand-up and key rituals; async follow-up between sessions.

blocks.title: Technical foundations
blocks.lede: Technical PM/PO: product, design, business logic, and stack aligned with your developers, AI prototypes before build.
items: Ceremonies and rituals with the dev team
Debt and risks visible to product
Laravel · React · Next.js as in your stack

items.title: We already have an « implicit » head of product (the founder)
items.content: Common, and draining. My role is to structure what the founder already carries: backlog, quarter, prerequisites, rituals, so you keep vision without being the bottleneck every morning.

items.title: Why half-days instead of full days?
items.content: To match real rhythm: stand-up and unblocking in the morning, prerequisite or prioritization workshops in the afternoon, without paying a full day when a half-day is enough. A full day lands when needed, in practice at most once a week, not as a routine.

items.title: Four half-days a week, isn't that almost full-time?
items.content: No: it's not four full days every week, that rhythm I turn down. Roughly two days of presence, split into focused half-days. Versus an employee: no employer costs, adjustable calendar, and a full day only when prototyping or validation warrants it.

items.title: What does a « technical » PM/PO change?
items.content: I speak development, business logic, and product in one conversation. Your devs don't translate fuzzy specs; the business doesn't pick between « pretty slides » and « buildable ». With AI, I can ship credible prototypes fast because I understand both sides.

items.title: AI prototypes aren't « real » product
items.content: Exactly: the goal is to reduce risk before code. A clickable flow or minimal POC lets you say no early, yes with confidence, not replace production delivery.

items.title: What if we hire a PM/PO later?
items.content: Even better if you arrive with roadmap, rituals, and ready-to-build standards already proven. I can help define the role and hand off to the internal hire.

items.title: Reachable outside scheduled days, or in an emergency?
items.content:
The agreed rhythm (e.g. half-days aligned to your rituals) structures deep work, not whether you can reach me.

Ongoing, I make time to reply on Slack or your preferred channel. If an emergency lands outside scheduled slots, I stay reachable and step in under a shared urgency frame we define up front (scope, priority, response time), not a vague 24/7 on-call promise.

blocks.label: Discuss PM/PO need
blocks.teaser: Need a technical PM/PO without full-time? A 30-minute call is enough to see if regular half-days, full day when needed, product + code + AI prototypes, fits your team.
